title: Hicorius oliviformis
taxonomy: Hicorius oliviformis is classified in the family Sapindaceae, which is a family of flowering plants commonly known as the soapberry family. This family includes over 130 genera and approximately 1,500 species. The Sapindaceae family is divided into several subfamilies and tribes, with Hicorius oliviformis belonging to the subfamily Sapindoideae. The subfamily Sapindoideae is further divided into several tribes, with the tribe Sapindae being the closest relative to Hicorius oliviformis. Further classification of Hicorius oliviformis is still a subject of ongoing research.

parts: Hicorius oliviformis, also known as the American Persimmon, is a plant that has been utilized for various medicinal purposes. The fruit, bark, and leaves of the plant are reported to have medicinal properties, although specific details about their uses are limited. preparations: Hicorius oliviformis, commonly known as the water hickory or bitternut hickory, is not a widely studied plant, and there is limited information available on its herbal preparations. However, the bark of the Hicorius oliviformis tree is sometimes used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments. The bark is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, making it a potential treatment for skin conditions and wounds. intro:
I couldn't find any information on a plant called "Hicorius oliviformis". However, I did find information on the Hicoria genus, particularly Hicoria oliviformis, also known as the Shagbark Hickory.

Here's a paragraph on the Shagbark Hickory plant:

Hicoria oliviformis, commonly known as the Shagbark Hickory, is a deciduous tree species native to eastern North America, characterized by its distinctive shaggy bark and edible nuts. The Shagbark Hickory has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of the Shagbark Hickory include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the Shagbark Hickory is often made into teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as used in traditional remedies for its perceived health benefits.
